sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Mr. Cook, a 60-year-old right-handed male, presents with numbness and tingling in both hands, which started a few weeks after a car accident in December.
He reports that the symptoms worsened over the last month, particularly in the right hand, and are accompanied by mild stiffness in the neck.
He also mentions soreness, throbbing, and weakness in the hands, especially when using them, with tingling more pronounced when the hands hurt.
The symptoms are more noticeable at night and come and go throughout the day.
He denies any loss of sensation in his hands or fingers.
He has a history of hypertension, anxiety, and prediabetes, which he controlled through diet changes.
He is taking Coreg for hypertension and Lexapro for anxiety.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of hypertension, anxiety, and prediabetes, which he has managed with diet changes.
He also has a history of a car accident in December, which resulted in a contusion to the head and fractures to both wrists.

Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, always being hungry, nail bed changes, dryness, and itching, as well as gout and muscle pain.
Current medications: I prescribed Coreg for hypertension, Lexapro for anxiety.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's sensation is normal, except for pin prick sensation diminished in the third and fourth fingers on the right and decreased in the fourth and fifth fingers on the left.
Reflexes are 1+ and symmetric, 2+ knees, and toes are downgoing bilaterally.
The Tinel sign is positive at both wrists and both elbows.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, potentially due to his past trauma, causing pain and tingling in both hands.
Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; cubital tunnel syndrome; hypertension; anxiety; prediabetes.
Plan:
I prescribed an EMG test to determine the exact location of the nerve irritation.
I will schedule this test for the patient and instruct him to return for a follow-up visit after the test results are available.
If symptoms persist, I will consider a referral to the hand clinic for injections or carpal tunnel release surgery or refer him to an orthopedist for the same treatment.
I advised the patient to continue wearing his splints.
Follow-up: I will see the patient after the EMG test results are available.
